outcome_contract.why: The prime app includes a messaging database for storing guest messages. The database ID in the deployment config is a placeholder that was never replaced with a real one. Any feature that reads or writes messages will not work in production.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: The real Cloudflare D1 database ID is in wrangler.toml, migrations have been applied, and the PRIME_MESSAGING_DB binding resolves correctly at runtime.

engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Would change error states in messaging UI

engineering_coverage.applicable: D1 binding is server-side only; no new exposure
Existing tests with in-memory D1 mock continue to pass; no new tests needed
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Existing tests pass; no new tests

engineering_coverage.applicable: New D1 schema via 4 migrations; well-defined contract
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Schema fully defined across 4 migrations; plan must apply all in order

engineering_coverage.applicable: Standard D1 for this schema size; no concerns
Create DB → apply migrations → update wrangler.toml; rollback = delete DB + revert wrangler.toml
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Clear rollback path; DB deletion + wrangler.toml revert

stage_payload.decision_summary: The prime guest app's messaging system has a fully-written application layer (repositories, API functions, type definitions, tests) but the backing Cloudflare D1 database was never created. The `wrangler.toml` has a placeholder `database_id = "000...000"`. The decision to make here is: how do we restore messaging functionality — create the real database, or remove/gate the dependency?
goals: Messaging persistence operational in production (PRIME_MESSAGING_DB binding resolves)
All 4 existing migrations applied in order
wrangler.toml carries the real database ID
non_goals: Changes to Cloudflare Pages dashboard binding (wrangler.toml is source of truth)
constraints: Cloudflare Pages deployment (`wrangler pages dev` picks up `[[d1_databases]]`)
Migration files are in `wrangler d1 migrations` format
assumptions: Account-scoped CF token has D1 create/migrate write permission

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Core goal — current state is broken
Avoids scope creep and re-testing burden
Production infra changes should be reversible

options_considered.Description: Run `wrangler d1 create prime-messaging`, apply 4 migrations, write real ID to wrangler.toml
options_considered.Upside: Fully restores messaging; no app code changes; uses existing migration toolchain
options_considered.Downside: Requires CF token to have D1 create/migrate scope — same token manages Pages + DNS, so assumed sufficient (low risk)
options_considered.Key risks: Token scope assumed; if `wrangler d1 create` fails with auth error, elevate token scope or use CF dashboard

options_considered.Description: Delete D1 binding from wrangler.toml and remove application code that references it
options_considered.Downside: Destroys implemented messaging system; large code deletion; messaging functionality lost entirely
options_considered.Key risks: Permanent loss of delivered work; reversal would require re-implementing

options_considered.Option: C — Gate behind feature flag, defer DB creation
options_considered.Description: Add runtime feature flag; suppress all messaging errors
options_considered.Downside: Hides a broken system behind a flag; adds flag debt; still requires DB creation before any real use; messaging remains non-functional in production
options_considered.Key risks: Increases technical debt; defers the same 4-step fix indefinitely

chosen_approach.Recommendation: Option A — Create the `prime-messaging` D1 database, apply all 4 migration files in order, and update `apps/prime/wrangler.toml` with the real database ID.
chosen_approach.Why this wins: All application code is already complete and tested. The only missing piece is the infrastructure. This is the minimal, reversible fix that makes messaging operational with zero application code changes. Option B destroys working code. Option C hides the problem without fixing it.
chosen_approach.What it depends on: CF API token having D1 create/migrate scope (same token manages Pages + DNS — low risk). `wrangler` CLI accessible in the build environment.
chosen_approach.**Option B (remove binding)** — permanen: the application code is complete, tested, and waiting on infrastructure.

rejected_approaches: **Option B (remove binding)** — permanently destroys a fully-implemented messaging system. Rejection is unambiguous: the application code is complete, tested, and waiting on infrastructure.
**Option C (feature flag)** — adds flag debt without eliminating the broken state. The fix is the same 4 steps regardless; a flag only defers it. Rejected as wasteful.

validation_implications: Existing unit tests pass without changes (in-memory D1 mock is unaffected)
CHECKPOINT verification is the primary acceptance signal
sequencing_constraints: TASK-01 must complete before TASK-02 (need DB to apply migrations)
TASK-03 must complete before TASK-04 (need real ID in wrangler.toml to verify binding)
TASK-02 and TASK-03 can be combined or run in either order after TASK-01

areas.label: PRIME_MESSAGING_DB binding
areas.trigger: Any CF Pages deploy / local `wrangler pages dev`
areas.future: `wrangler d1 create` → real DB ID captured → written to wrangler.toml → `wrangler d1 migrations apply prime-messaging --remote` → all 4 migrations applied → binding resolves at runtime
areas.steady: All application code unchanged; existing tests unchanged
areas.seams: Token D1 write scope assumed; Pages project name match assumed

areas.label: Messaging API functions
areas.trigger: Guest or staff request to any messaging endpoint
areas.future: `hasPrimeMessagingDb()` returns true; `getPrimeMessagingDb()` returns live D1 instance; repository functions execute against real SQLite
areas.steady: API function signatures, guards, and business logic unchanged

areas.label: Migration state
areas.future: Migrations 0001–0004 applied in sequence; all 8 tables + indexes present
areas.seams: Migration ordering must be enforced; apply one-at-a-time or `--remote` applies all pending in order
